us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With Strikes, Refuses to Rule Out Ground Troops
us-politics.body:
Trump spent Easter Sunday issuing profane threats against Iran on social media, warning of strikes on power plants and bridges if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened. He declined to rule out ground troops if Tehran refuses a deal, while a former adviser warned the escalating rhetoric makes any agreement less likely. Separately, the US rescued a downed service member from behind enemy lines inside Iran, with the CIA reportedly running a deception campaign to assist the operation.

Opinion is split on strategy. A top House Republican argued the only legitimate ground-troop mission would be seizing enriched uranium. A senior House Democrat countered that Iran's ability to close the Strait poses a greater strategic threat than its nuclear program. Critics on the left point to cost and mission creep, while some on the right back sustained military pressure to force a deal.

sources.title: Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon
Inside the daring rescue of airman behind enemy lines: How CIA assisted with 'deception campaign'
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump
Former World Bank chief says Iran economy collapsing into ‘barter system’ amid conflict

us-politics.headline: Bondi Firing Rattles Washington; Trump Denies Broader Cabinet Shake-Up
us-politics.body:
Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, then told reporters no further Cabinet changes are coming. Democrats moved quickly to frame the firings as a warning about executive accountability, with Senator Kaine calling Bondi's dismissal a lesson for whoever takes the job next. NPR reported Trump is simultaneously seeking to walk back several campaign promises.

House Minority Leader Jeffries used the moment to press Speaker Johnson to cut short the House recess, citing a lapse in DHS funding that the firings have left unresolved. The back-to-back dismissals have renewed debate about the durability of Trump's second-term inner circle and the independence of the Justice Department.

sources.title: Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings
Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee
Senate Democrat says Trump ‘needs a lot of prayer’
Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises
Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse

us-politics.headline: NATO Fractures as Iran War Strains US Alliances
us-politics.body:
The conflict with Iran is accelerating tensions inside NATO. Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace has drawn attention as a sign that the alliance's cohesion is fraying under pressure from Washington's unilateral posture. Analysts describe a shift toward what some are calling a post-global NATO, where bilateral relationships replace collective commitments.

Trump's approach has unsettled European partners who were not consulted before military action began. The Strait of Hormuz crisis is also sharpening the domestic debate over energy policy, with some arguing the conflict exposes US vulnerability to oil supply disruptions and strengthens the case for diversifying energy sources.

sources.title: Trump roils NATO as pressure builds over Strait of Hormuz
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances
The war in Iran is showing why the US needs diverse energy resources

us-politics.headline: Democrats Eye 2028, Debate How to Rebuild With Voters
us-politics.body:
Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego said he is open to a 2028 presidential run, telling reporters the party has to look at every option to recapture the White House. Representative Ro Khanna argued Democrats are making a strategic error by refusing to engage with large online audiences, pointing to left-leaning streamer Hasan Piker as an example of reach the party is leaving on the table.

A separate opinion piece warned that Democrats have not yet resolved the tension between their affordability messaging and their policy record, a gap that could hurt them in the midterms. The internal debate reflects a party still working out whether its problems are about candidates, communication, or policy.

sources.title: Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’
‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Squeezes Drivers, Workers and Used Car Market
au-politics.body:
Rising petrol prices are rippling through the economy in unexpected ways. Servo staff are bearing the brunt of customer anger at the bowser, with operators also struggling to absorb margin pressure as wholesale costs climb. The stress is human as well as financial.

Meanwhile, the second-hand EV market is tightening fast. Buyers priced out of petrol are turning to used electric vehicles, pushing prices up and shrinking supply. Some sellers say holding onto a combustion engine no longer makes financial sense. The fuel crunch is accelerating a shift the industry expected to take years.

sources.title: Petrol station staff and operators bare brunt of fuel price rise stress
‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits

au-politics.headline: Iran Tests Limits in Hormuz as US-China Tensions Sharpen
au-politics.body:
Iran's move to treat the Strait of Hormuz as a de facto tollbooth for shipping marks a potential turning point in regional maritime security. Analysts warn it could set a precedent that emboldens other actors to weaponise strategic waterways. The stakes for global trade are significant.

Separately, US intelligence has accused China of sharing satellite imagery of American military bases with Iran, a claim that deepens concerns about coordination between the two powers. The Spectator notes Iran has also signalled openness to talks with the Trump administration, suggesting Tehran is playing multiple tracks at once - pressure and diplomacy simultaneously.

sources.title: US intelligence says Chinese satellite imagery of bases is helping Iran
Iran's 'maritime tollbooth' could be start of dangerous new era
Iran has offered Trump an olive branch

au-politics.headline: Aboriginal Child Removal Case Exposes Ongoing Welfare Tensions
au-politics.body:
A family court has ruled that an Aboriginal child moved 1,700 kilometres from their remote Northern Territory community must be returned. The case highlights persistent tensions between child welfare agencies and Indigenous families over removal decisions, cultural connection and the long shadow of past policies.

The ruling reinforces that courts will weigh cultural ties heavily in placement decisions. Critics argue the system still defaults too readily to removal rather than community-based support. The case is unlikely to be the last of its kind.

sources.title: Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: Swift Parrot Habitat Logged Despite Recorded Presence
au-politics.body:
A patch of Tasmanian forest where the critically endangered swift parrot was recorded calling dozens of times has been logged. Conservation groups say the case illustrates the gap between wildlife monitoring and on-the-ground protections, with data collected but not acted on in time.

The swift parrot is one of Australia's most at-risk birds, its population already under severe pressure from habitat loss. Advocates are calling for stronger legal mechanisms to halt logging when endangered species are detected. The Tasmanian forestry industry has not publicly responded to the specific case.

sources.title: The swift parrot’s distinctive call was recorded dozens of times in a patch of Tasmanian forest. Then the forest was logged

world.headline: Israel, Iran Trade Strikes as Hormuz Closure Shocks World
world.body:
Israel struck targets in Iran and Lebanon while Iranian missiles hit a residential building in Haifa and an industrial zone in southern Israel, marking one of the most intense exchange of fire since the conflict escalated. At least 14 people were killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon. Iran has now shut down internet access for a record-breaking stretch, deepening frustration among its population.

President Trump threatened Iran with 'hell' over the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, but offered no concrete plan to reopen it - a gap that alarmed European leaders. OPEC+ announced an output hike but warned of slow recovery. Nepal introduced two-day weekends as a fuel crisis bites. Iran's former foreign minister Zarif floated a peace roadmap, though Gulf states signalled eroded trust.

sources.title: Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa
Israeli air strikes, shelling across Lebanon add to rising death toll
OPEC+ agrees to hike oil output, warns of slow recovery after attacks
At least 14 people killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel
Trump threatens ‘hell’ for Iran over Hormuz Strait as deadline approaches
Mearsheimer: No signs of quick end to US-Israel war on Iran
Nepal announces two-day weekends as fuel crisis caused by Iran war deepens
Trump offers no concrete plan as Strait of Hormuz closure shocks Europe
Iran’s ex-FM Zarif proposes peace roadmap; Gulf points at erosion of trust

world.headline: Downed US Airman Rescued from Inside Iran
world.body:
A seriously wounded US airman was rescued from inside Iran after his F-15 was shot down, President Trump confirmed. Details of the rescue operation emerged Sunday, describing a covert extraction carried out under active conflict conditions. The incident underlines how directly US forces are now engaged in the Iran conflict.

Trump's confirmation came as political scientist John Mearsheimer told Al Jazeera there are no signs of a quick end to the US-Israel war on Iran. Iran's sports minister separately said the country's participation in the 2026 World Cup depends on matches being relocated away from the United States.

sources.title: How downed F-15 US airman was rescued inside Iran
Seriously wounded US airman rescued from Iran, Trump says
Mearsheimer: No signs of quick end to US-Israel war on Iran
Iran’s World Cup participation depends on relocation from US, minister says

world.headline: Gaza Deteriorates Further as Easter Passes in Silence
world.body:
Christians in Gaza refused to mark Easter, with community members describing celebrations as impossible amid ongoing bombardment and what they called a genocide. Tent camps across the territory are now facing rodent infestations, adding disease risk to an already catastrophic humanitarian situation. Israeli minister Ben-Gvir outlined plans for a Palestinian 'death row', drawing fresh condemnation.

Pope Leo used his first Easter Mass to call on world leaders to choose peace. Pope Francis separately warned that the world is growing indifferent to violence. In Lebanon, Christian families spent Easter displaced by Israeli strikes.

sources.title: Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide
Pope warns world is becoming ‘indifferent’ to violence
Israel’s Ben-Gvir details plans for Palestinian ‘death row’
Christian Lebanese families displaced as they celebrate Easter
Pope Leo calls for global leaders to choose peace in his first Easter Mass

world.headline: Pipeline Bomb Plot Found; Mediterranean Migrant Boat Capsizes
world.body:
Serbia and Hungary say explosives were discovered on a Russian gas pipeline, with Hungary alleging a deliberate plot timed ahead of its upcoming election. No group has claimed responsibility. The incident raises fresh concerns about infrastructure sabotage in Central Europe.

Separately, dozens of migrants are missing after a boat capsized in the Mediterranean. A suspected measles outbreak in Bangladesh has killed nearly 100 children. And NASA's Artemis II crew sent back striking images of Earth and the Moon as the mission continued its lunar flyby.

sources.title: Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline
Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Dozens missing after migrant boat capsizes in Mediterranean
Earth in rear-view of Artemis II astronauts
Artemis's stunning Moon pictures - science or holiday photos?
Suspected measles outbreak kills nearly 100 children in Bangladesh

ai.headline: Microsoft calls Copilot entertainment, AI legal liability grows
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now classify Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only" - a quiet but significant legal maneuver that limits the company's liability when the AI produces wrong or harmful outputs. It signals how seriously tech firms are hedging against AI accountability, even as they aggressively market these tools for professional use.

Suno faces similar scrutiny on a different front. The AI music platform is generating copyright headaches by producing tracks that closely mimic real artists, raising unresolved questions about training data and commercial reproduction rights. Together, these cases show the legal scaffolding around AI products is still being built in real time, and companies are protecting themselves first.

sources.title: Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use
Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: AI coding tools multiply as pricing and local options expand
ai.body:
OpenAI is shifting Codex pricing from per-message to per-token, aligning costs with actual API usage and making the tool more predictable for developers building on top of it. The change reflects growing pressure to compete on economics as the AI coding market gets crowded.

On the open-source side, Google's Gemma 4 is now running locally via LM Studio's new headless CLI, with developers pairing it alongside Claude Code for hybrid workflows. Meanwhile, a project called Nanocode is pushing Claude Code onto TPUs using pure JAX, squeezing maximum performance from a $200 budget. Local and low-cost AI development is accelerating fast.

sources.title: Running Google Gemma 4 Locally with LM Studio's New Headless CLI and Claude Code
Codex pricing to align with API token usage, instead of per-message
Nanocode: The best Claude Code that $200 can buy in pure JAX on TPUs

ai.headline: Japan's robots fill labor gaps as Gemini navigates city streets
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots not to replace workers but to fill roles that simply go unfilled, driven by an aging population and chronic labor shortages. The country is becoming a live test bed for real-world robotics at scale, with results that are outpacing lab expectations.

On the consumer side, Google's Gemini integration in Maps is earning cautious praise after hands-on testing showed it handling day-planning tasks competently. It is a small but telling sign that AI assistants are moving from novelty to utility in everyday navigation and scheduling.

sources.title: I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

ai.headline: Digital wallets reach displaced Lebanese as CBP codes leak online
ai.body:
Lebanon is distributing humanitarian aid through digital wallets to over one million displaced people, using mobile infrastructure where traditional banking has collapsed. The approach is being watched closely as a model for crisis-zone fintech deployment.

In a separate security lapse, codes for US Customs and Border Protection facilities appear to have leaked through publicly accessible online flashcard sets, likely created by employees studying for internal certifications. It is a reminder that sensitive operational data often escapes through mundane, low-tech channels rather than sophisticated breaches.

sources.title: With one million displaced, Lebanon turns to digital wallets for aid
CBP facility codes sure seem to have leaked via online flashcards

btc.headline: Bitcoin Holds at $67K as Sentiment Turns Deeply Bearish
btc.body: Bitcoin is stuck near $67,000 with market sentiment at its worst levels since the Iran war began, yet the price refuses to break down. Some traders are calling new lows a matter of time, pointing to stalled momentum and weak demand. The resilience may have a structural explanation. Spot ETFs have changed how Bitcoin responds to macro signals - rather than reacting to Federal Reserve decisions, Bitcoin now front-runs them, as institutional flows price in rate expectations weeks in advance. That shift insulates the asset from the kind of sharp reactive selloffs that once followed Fed announcements. Robert Kiyosaki is using the uncertainty to push his familiar case for Bitcoin and gold, drawing parallels to the 1974 breakdown of dollar-backed monetary norms.

sources.title: Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause
Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle
Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K

btc.headline: North Korea Ran Six-Month Operation Behind $270M Drift Exploit
btc.body: The $270 million hack of Solana-based exchange Drift was not an opportunistic attack. Drift says it was a deliberate, six-month intelligence operation run by North Korean state actors. The timeline suggests the attackers spent months mapping the protocol before striking. The revelation lands as Ledger's CTO warns that AI is actively worsening crypto's security landscape, lowering the cost and skill threshold for sophisticated attacks. Together, the two stories paint a grim picture - state-level adversaries are growing more patient and precise, while AI tools are simultaneously arming a broader pool of attackers.

sources.title: AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation

btc.headline: Ant Group Builds Crypto Rails for AI Agents to Transact
btc.body: Ant Group's blockchain division has launched a platform letting AI agents conduct transactions directly on crypto infrastructure. The move positions autonomous software as a first-class economic actor - able to pay for services, execute contracts, and move value without human sign-off. It is one of the clearest signals yet that the AI-crypto convergence is moving from concept to product. Ant Group's scale gives the project immediate credibility, though questions around accountability and regulatory treatment of agent-initiated transactions remain wide open.

sources.title: Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails

btc.headline: Token Oversupply Is Becoming an Existential Threat to Crypto
btc.body: A Blockworks executive is calling token oversupply an existential problem for the industry. New tokens are hitting the market faster than genuine value is being created to support them, diluting returns and eroding investor confidence. The critique cuts at a core tension in crypto - the ease of launching tokens has always been a feature, but at scale it becomes a liability. This dynamic also complicates the Bitcoin-dollar relationship that a Bitcoin Policy Institute executive described as symbiotic, since a flood of low-quality tokens muddies the broader narrative around crypto as a legitimate asset class.

sources.title: Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec
‘Existential’ token problem seen as supply outpaces value creation: Blockworks exec

food.name: Mafaldine al Limone e Bottarga
food.description: Ruffled ribbon pasta tossed in brown butter, lemon zest, and shaved cured mullet roe for a briny, citrus-bright hit
food.tip: Grate the bottarga over the pasta off the heat — direct heat turns it bitter and kills the oceanic depth you're paying for

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that builds through four 8-minute blocks at progressively faster paces — easy, moderate, comfortably hard, and race-effort — with 2-minute walk recoveries between each, training your body to shift gears rather than just log miles

learn.description: A trophic cascade is the chain reaction triggered when a single predator is added to or removed from an ecosystem — most famously demonstrated when wolves were reintroduced to Yellowstone in 1995 and inadvertently changed the course of rivers by altering elk grazing behaviour.
learn.why: It's a masterclass in second-order thinking and unintended consequences, and once you understand it you start seeing cascade logic everywhere — in economies, organisations, and social networks — not just in nature
